title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Triggers Childhood Wounds
meta_description: Discover why spiritual awakening often brings up childhood wounds. Direct insight on healing, not fluff. Read more.
og_title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Triggers Childhood Wounds
og_description: Discover why spiritual awakening often brings up childhood wounds. Direct insight on healing, not fluff. Read more.
body:
You're Not Falling Apart. You're Waking Up.

Let me guess. You started feeling something shift in your life. Maybe it was a quiet nudge at first. A sense that the old routines, the old relationships, the old version of you just didn't fit anymore. Then the nudge became a shove. And now? It feels like everything is breaking open. You're having flashes of clarity, moments of deep peace, but also... waves of raw, aching, old pain. Pain you thought you'd buried long ago.

You're not broken. You're not regressing. You're experiencing something very specific, and very misunderstood: a spiritual awakening that is actively flushing out your childhood wounds. And if no one told you this was going to happen, you're probably wondering what the hell is wrong with you. Nothing is wrong with you. This is the work. This is the bright wound.

I'm Kalesh, and I talk to people like you every day. People whose nervous systems are wired for depth. People who feel too much, remember too much, and have been told their whole lives to "calm down" or "stop being so sensitive." You're not too sensitive. You're calibrated for a wider range than most. And when you start to wake up spiritually, that calibration gets turned up to eleven. That's when the old stuff comes roaring back.

"The spiritual path is not about avoiding your wounds. It's about walking directly into them with your eyes open, knowing that the light on the other side is brighter than the darkness you're passing through."

Why Does This Happen? The Nervous System Connection

Think of your childhood wounds as knots in your energy field. Knots in your nervous system. When you were young, you learned to survive. You learned to shut down parts of yourself to stay safe. You learned to hold your breath, to make yourself small, to please others, to dissociate when things got too heavy. These weren't mistakes. They were brilliant survival strategies. They kept you alive.

But here's the thing. Spiritual awakening is a process of expansion. It's your soul saying, "Hey, you don't need those survival strategies anymore. You're safe now. Let's open up." And when you open up, all the stuff you stuffed down starts to rise. It's not a punishment. It's a clearing. Your nervous system is finally safe enough to release what it's been holding for decades.

Does that land? Because this is the part most people miss. They think spiritual awakening is supposed to be all bliss and light. And it can be. But the real deal? The kind of awakening that actually changes your life? It comes with a detox. And the detox is your unprocessed childhood pain.

If you want to understand the mechanics of this more deeply, I highly recommend checking out The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not a "spiritual" book per se, but it explains exactly how trauma lives in the body and how releasing it is a physical, not just mental, process. See it as a companion piece to your awakening.

The Three Wounds That Always Surface

In my years of working with sensitive people, I've noticed a pattern. When spiritual awakening kicks in, three specific childhood wounds tend to surface. Not everyone gets all three, but most people get at least one. See if any of these hit home for you.

1. The Abandonment Wound

This one is huge. It's the feeling that you'll be left behind. That people you love will disappear. That you're fundamentally alone. This wound often comes from early experiences where a parent was physically or emotionally unavailable. Maybe they worked too much. Maybe they were depressed. Maybe they just couldn't see you.

When you start to awaken, you might find yourself terrified of losing your spiritual community, your partner, or even your sense of connection to Source. You might cling to teachers or groups. You might feel a deep panic when you're alone with your own energy. That's the abandonment wound saying, "Don't leave me. I can't survive alone."

But here's the truth. You can survive alone. You can thrive alone. And the awakening is actually teaching you that your connection to the divine is unbreakable. No one can take it from you. No one can leave and take your light with them. It's yours. Always has been.

2. The Betrayal Wound

This one shows up as hyper-vigilance. You're constantly scanning for who's going to hurt you next. You trust no one fully. You expect people to lie, to cheat, to let you down. This wound often comes from childhood experiences where someone you trusted broke that trust in a big way. A parent who promised something and didn't deliver. A caregiver who was unpredictable. An adult who should have protected you but didn't.

During awakening, this wound can make you suspicious of spiritual teachers, of your own guidance, of the universe itself. You might wonder if this whole awakening thing is just another trick. Another promise that won't deliver. Right?!

The healing here is learning to trust yourself first. Before you trust anyone else. Before you trust the universe. You learn that you can handle whatever comes. You learn that your own inner knowing is stronger than any external betrayal. And slowly, you start to let people in again. But this time, you keep your boundaries strong.

3. The Shame Wound

This is the deepest one. It's the feeling that something is fundamentally wrong with you. That you're bad. That you're broken beyond repair. This wound often comes from being criticized, shamed, or punished for being yourself as a child. Maybe you were told you were too much. Maybe you were told to stop crying. Maybe you were ignored when you needed help.

When spiritual awakening hits, the shame wound can make you feel unworthy of spiritual connection. You might feel like a fraud. Like you're pretending to be enlightened. Like everyone else is further along than you. You might compare yourself endlessly. You might feel like you're doing it wrong.

Know what I mean? This is the wound that tells you you're not good enough to be loved by God. But that's a lie. The awakening is here to show you that you are worthy exactly as you are. Not after you fix yourself. Not after you heal. Right now. In the middle of the mess.

For working with the shame wound specifically, I often recommend The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. It's a practical, grounded guide to letting go of who you think you're supposed to be and embracing who you actually are. Worth every page.

Why Your Sensitivity Makes This Worse (And Better)

If you're reading this, you're probably a highly sensitive person. You pick up on energy. You feel other people's emotions. You have a rich inner world. You're deeply intuitive. And that sensitivity is exactly why you're having a spiritual awakening in the first place. Sensitive people are the canaries in the coal mine. You feel the shift before anyone else does.

But that sensitivity also means you feel your childhood wounds more intensely. When the abandonment wound surfaces, you don't just feel a little sad. You feel like you're drowning. When the shame wound shows up, you don't just feel embarrassed. You feel like you want to disappear. Your nervous system is wide open, and it's processing decades of stored pain all at once.

This is not a flaw. This is your design. Your sensitivity is what allows you to heal deeply. Someone with a thicker nervous system might never feel these wounds enough to clear them. They stay stuck. But you? You feel everything. And that means you can move through it. You can release it. You can transform it.

The key is learning to work with your nervous system instead of against it. You need tools. You need practices. You need to know that the waves of pain are not permanent. They are passing. They are clearing.

How to Work With the Wounds When They Surface

So what do you do when the old pain comes up? Here's a practical approach. No fluff. Just what works.

Stop Trying to "Fix" It

The first instinct is to get rid of the pain. To heal it. To transcend it. But that's resistance. And resistance makes it worse. Instead, try this. When the wound surfaces, just sit with it. Don't analyze it. Don't try to figure out where it came from. Just feel it in your body. Where is it? In your chest? Your throat? Your stomach? What does it feel like? Heavy? Hot? Cold? Just be with it.

You're not trying to make it go away. You're showing it that you're safe now. You're an adult. You can handle this. The child inside you needs to know that you're here. That you're not going to abandon them. That you're not going to shame them. That you're going to stay with them until the wave passes.

And it will pass. Every time. It might take five minutes. It might take an hour. But it always passes. And each time you do this, you're rewiring your nervous system. You're teaching it that these feelings are not threats. They are just energy moving through.

Use Your Intuition to Guide You

You have an inner knowing. Trust it. When a wound surfaces, ask yourself: "What do I need right now?" Maybe you need to cry. Maybe you need to scream into a pillow. Maybe you need to wrap yourself in a blanket and drink tea. Maybe you need to move your body. Your intuition knows. Listen to it.

This is where your sensitivity becomes your superpower. You can feel what you need. Most people can't. They're too numb. But you're not numb. You're awake. And being awake means you can respond to your own needs with compassion and precision.

For more on using your intuition as a healing tool, I recommend The Intuitive Way by Penney Peirce. It's a deep dive into how to trust your inner guidance, especially when things get messy.

Don't Do It Alone

This is critical. Spiritual awakening is not meant to be a solo journey. You need people who get it. People who understand that the pain is part of the process. People who won't tell you to "just think positive" or "just let it go." Find a community. Find a teacher. Find a therapist who works with spiritual emergence.

You don't have to explain yourself to people who don't understand. You just need a few people who do. And if you don't have that yet, keep looking. They're out there. I promise.

The Bright Wound: Why This Pain Is a Gift

There's a reason I call this "The Bright Wound." Because the wounds that surface during spiritual awakening are not just pain. They are portals. They are the places where your light is trying to break through. The wound is where the light enters.

Think about it. The parts of you that were wounded as a child are the parts of you that were most alive. They were the parts that loved deeply. That trusted openly. That felt fully. The wound happened because someone or something shut that down. But the aliveness is still there. It's just buried under layers of protection.

When the wound surfaces during awakening, it's not just the pain coming up. It's the aliveness. It's the love. It's the joy that was frozen in time. The pain is the shell. Inside is a pearl. And you have to crack the shell to get to the pearl.

Does that land? Because this is the most important thing I can tell you. Your childhood wounds are not your enemy. They are the path. They are the curriculum. They are the exact places where your soul chose to grow. And now, as an adult, you get to go back and reclaim what was lost. You get to reparent yourself. You get to give yourself the love you didn't get.

That's the spiritual awakening. Not escaping the human experience. But diving fully into it. Healing the past. Reclaiming your power. And coming out the other side with a depth of compassion and wisdom that you could never have accessed any other way.

For a beautiful guide on reparenting yourself through the awakening process, check out The Emotion Code by Dr. Bradley Nelson. It offers a clear approach for identifying and releasing trapped emotions from childhood.

A Practical Practice for When the Wounds Hit

Let me leave you with something you can use right now. The next time a childhood wound surfaces during your awakening, try this three-step practice.

Step One: Name It. Say out loud: "This is my abandonment wound. It's old. It's not the present moment." Or "This is my shame wound. It's a ghost. It has no power over me now." Naming it takes it out of the shadows and puts it in the light.

Step Two: Feel It. Put your hand on the part of your body where you feel the pain. Breathe into it. Don't try to change it. Just let it be there. Say to yourself: "I am safe. I am an adult. I can handle this feeling."

Step Three: Ask It What It Needs. This is the key. The wound isn't just pain. It's a part of you that needs something. Ask it: "What do you need from me right now?" And then listen. The answer might surprise you. It might need to be held. It might need to be heard. It might need you to set a boundary. Trust what comes.

Do this every time. Not just once. Every time. Because healing is not a one-and-done event. It's a practice. It's a relationship. It's you showing up for yourself, again and again, until the wound finally feels safe enough to let go.

You're Exactly Where You Need to Be

I know this is hard. I know it feels like you're being torn apart sometimes. But you're not falling apart. You're falling open. And what's coming through the cracks is your true self. The self that was always there, waiting to be seen.

Your childhood wounds are not a sign that you're broken. They're a sign that you're human. And your spiritual awakening is not a sign that you're special. It's a sign that you're ready. Ready to heal. Ready to love. Ready to live the life you came here to live.

Keep going. You're doing it right. Even when it hurts. Especially when it hurts. Because that's where the gold is. That's where the bright wound opens, and the light pours in.
